title: Britain's New Leader Could Bring New Hope
gist: Gavekal's Anatole Kaletsky turns contrarian on UK assets exactly one decade after Brexit, arguing Keir Starmer's near-certain removal—triggered by Labour's catastrophic local election losses and Andy Burnham's landslide victory in a Manchester by-election—marks an inflection point that justifies optimism for UK asset investors.

themes.theme: UK Political Inflection and Asset Opportunity

insights.claim: Gavekal argues Starmer's removal became 'near certain' after Labour suffered 'catastrophic losses' in nationwide local elections in early May 2026, followed by Andy Burnham's landslide win in a Manchester suburban by-election—making a new UK prime minister by September at the latest.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: UK political uncertainty is resolving in a specific direction: a new Labour leader seen as more growth-oriented than Starmer, which markets may price as a modest positive for UK equities and gilts.
insights.quote: The removal of Sir Keir Starmer, Britain's most unpopular prime minister since the start of modern polling, has been almost inevitable since early May
insights.locator: The removal of Sir Keir Starmer

insights.claim: Gavekal's Kaletsky turns contrarian on UK assets exactly one decade after the Brexit referendum justified 'unalloyed pessimism'—arguing the current moment is appropriate to 'turn contrarian and offer some optimistic answers' to investors in UK assets.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Gavekal's contrarian inflection call on UK assets is a potential positioning signal; the Brexit discount that has persisted in UK equities relative to global peers may be starting to unwind.
insights.quote: Exactly a decade after the Brexit referendum justified unalloyed pessimism about Britain, it now seems appropriate to turn contrarian
insights.locator: Exactly a decade after the Brexit referendum
